text.title: JTBD Interview Method and Job Mapping
text.summary: Switcher interview technique, timeline reconstruction questions, 8-stage job map, and worked examples.

rule.statement: Interview people who recently purchased, switched to a competitor, or started using a category — not long-tenured loyal customers. Recent switchers remember the decision; loyal customers have rationalized it.
rule.rationale: Memory of the decision process fades within weeks; loyal customers reconstruct a coherent narrative that omits the actual friction and anxiety that drove the switch.

rule.statement: Ask "what did you use before?" before asking about the current product — the previous solution reveals the job better than the new one.
rule.rationale: The job is defined by what was inadequate, not by what the customer currently values. The switch happened because the old solution failed the job.

section.title: Timeline Reconstruction Questions
section.p: Reconstruct the decision timeline across six stages: First Thought (trigger), Passive Looking (awareness), Active Looking (evaluation), Decision (purchase), Consumption (usage), Satisfaction (review).

Key questions: "When did you first think you needed something different?" / "What was happening in your life at that point?" / "What did you try before this?" / "What almost stopped you from switching?"

rule.statement: For complex jobs, map all 8 stages: Define, Locate, Prepare, Confirm, Execute, Monitor, Modify, Conclude. For each stage: customer goals, current pain points, opportunity for the product to help. Build the product around the highest-pain stages, not the core execution stage alone.
rule.rationale: Most products address the Execute stage well but abandon the customer at Locate, Prepare, and Confirm — which are often the highest-friction points and the real differentiator.

example.note: Milkshake job (Christensen): Commuters buying milkshakes at 7am. Surface understanding: they want breakfast. JTBD: "When I have a long boring commute, I want something to do with my hand that lasts 20 minutes, so I can make the drive less boring." Real competition: donuts, bananas, bagels, podcasts. Implication: make milkshake thicker and chunkier — a product insight invisible from demographic analysis.

Online course JTBD: Surface = "learn Python." Real job: "When I am stuck in a dead-end job, I want to gain a skill that is in demand, so I can switch careers and earn more money." Real competition: bootcamps, MBA, networking for promotion, starting a business. A Python course competing on curriculum loses; one competing on career change wins.

antipattern.description: Asking "what features do you want?" instead of "what are you trying to accomplish?"
antipattern.reason: Feature requests describe a solution hypothesis; job discovery requires understanding the progress the customer is trying to make, which is often different from what they explicitly request.

antipattern.description: Building one job statement for all customers when different segments hire the product for different jobs.
antipattern.reason: A single product can be hired for multiple distinct jobs; conflating them into one statement produces positioning that speaks clearly to nobody.

text.reference: LLM prompt to extract JTBD statement from transcript
